topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email arrived with a sterile subject line: "Sample Essays for Undergraduate Applicants." My heart gave a sudden, unpleasant lurch. I clicked it, expecting a generic template, but what flooded my screen was my own meticulously crafted narrative, perfectly formatted, sitting beside a glowing commendation. It was published. As a third-year student drowning in self-doubt, staring at the screen, I felt a coldness seep into my bones. My hands began to tremble, the paper beneath my fingers suddenly feeling alien and heavy. I stared at the words—words that spoke of passion and insight—and couldn't reconcile them with the hollow ache in my chest. I crumpled the printout, the sound loud in the quiet dorm room, and shoved it deep into a drawer, hoping the evidence of my own supposed brilliance would simply vanish.

[story 2]

I found the file tucked away in a forgotten folder on my laptop, a misplaced relic from a past application cycle. When I opened it, the prose was undeniably mine. The anecdotes, the reflective turns, the way I managed to articulate complex feelings—it was flawless. A wave of nausea washed over me, making my vision swim. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and just sat there, unable to move. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I traced a line of text with my finger, the ink feeling impossibly real, and a deep, pervasive sense of inadequacy settled over me, heavy as wet wool. Why did this happen? Why did something so bright exist outside of my own unremarkable existence?

[story 3]

It was a Tuesday afternoon, and I was supposed to be studying organic chemistry, but instead, I was staring at a screen displaying my personal statement. A flicker of recognition, quickly followed by a sickening drop in my stomach, made me freeze. The essay was live, featured in some online admissions guide. The air seemed to thin out around me. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of my apartment, my footsteps echoing hollowly. My jaw tightened until my teeth ached. Every muscle in my body felt coiled, tense with a frantic, internal alarm. I walked to the window, looking out at the indifferent city, feeling profoundly disconnected, as if I were observing a performance I was no longer qualified to participate in.
